content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. 90 minutes edge of the seat thriller movies

**Score:** 113
**Body:** Suggest me few 90 minutes edge of the seat thriller movies as I am getting bored and home alone want to watch a good movie in short time.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. run lola run!
2. Phone Booth 2002
3. Red Eye (2005)
4. Green Room. Anxiety and tension starts five minutes in and doesn't stop until 90 minutes later. Highly recommend!
5. Breakdown 
6. Does Crank fit the bill?
7. Eden lake
8. The Hitcher (1986)
9. Duel
10. The Descent

## 2. Movies That Feel Like Summer

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Looking for movies that really capture that summer feeling like hanging out with friends, late nights, no responsibilities, just good vibes. Stuff like Dazed and Confused, Grown Ups, or anything with that fun, nostalgic, summertime energy. I’m not really looking for heavy plots more just movies that feel like summer when you watch them.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Way Way Back (2013)
2. Do the right thing
3. Stand By Me 
4. Adventureland
5. The Sandlot 
6. *The Way, Way Back*

Sam Rockwell is delightful. 

7. Dazed and Confused 
8. American Graffiti captures that vibe on the “last” day of summer for its main characters. 
9. Licorice Pizza, I can't recommend it enough. It's outstanding.
10. Summer Rental and Great Outdoors if you love John Candy. Also Caddyshack to throw in a golf element 

## 3. Movies about the good in humanity

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Please no spoilers, I like to go in blind.

I’m looking for movies that are about, at least enough that you’d suggest it here, the good in people and humanity. 

I’ll watch suggestions in any language or made at any time. 

Thank you to anyone who suggests a movie. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s a Wonderful Life
2. Big Fish is more about the good of being good to humanity and how it's good back, but a great watch
3. Harvey
4. Children of Men.
5. Hunt for the Wilderpeople
6. As Good as it Gets (1997)

A Man Called Otto (2022)

The Terminal (2004)
7. Pay it forward
8. Babette's Feast (1987)
9. Deep Impact
10. Our Daily Bread (1934)

The Straight Story (1999)

## 4. Movies with big twists in the middle of them, not just the end

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I love plot twists / moments that make you re-think what you've seen up until that point, but I don't like when they happen at the very end. I don't think about movies much after watching them, nor do I like rewatching movies, so final hour plot twists don't strike my fancy. I guess by "middle" though, I really just mean not the very end

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gone Girl
2. Sorry to Bother You. That movie took a crazy turn
 
A show not a movie, Sugar on Apple TV

3. Jojo Rabbit 
4. *The Place Beyond the Pines*

It becomes a completely different film at the halfway point. 

5. The Crying Game
6. Crazy Stupid Love

A Place Beyond the Pines
7. There’s a French movie that’s called  “À la folie... pas du tout”which translates to “He Loves Me, He Loves Me Not.” Pretty good mid movie twist in it. It’s a thriller if that’s your thing. 
8. Psycho

Father of My Children
9. From Dusk Till Dawn 
10. Vertigo

The Matrix's big twist was more like 20-30 minutes in.

## 5. Looking for rom-com movie recommendations! Something light, funny, and feel-good. Any favorites?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I’m in the mood for a good rom-com, but I want something that feels a bit different from the usual predictable storyline. I still want that “butterflies” feeling and great chemistry between the leads, just without it being overly cliché or cheesy.

Any recommendations? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When Harry Met Sally 
2. 50 first dates
3. crazy rich asians 🙂‍↕️
4. A Good Year(2006) directed by Ridley Scott and stars Russell Crowe. Fantastic feel good film with a dash of romance.
5. Fabulous Baker Boys 

Crossing Delancey

Blow Dry

One Night at McCool’s

Urban Cowboy (lord, I love this movie.  “Mama, don’t let your babies grow up to be cowboys!”)

High Society

And its source, Philadelphia Story
6. You’ve Got Mail (Tom Hanks, Meg Ryan)

Laws of Attraction (Pierce Brosnan, Julianne Moore) 
7. 10 things I hate about you, a lot like love 
8. *Always Be My Maybe* - Pretty good overall, but the extended cameo in the middle by Keanu Reeves as “himself” raises it to must-see status. Those scenes are like a bizarre, hilarious little movie-within-the-movie.

*Housesitter* - Steve Martin and Goldie Hawn. “You’re like the Ernest Hemingway of bullshit!”

*Enough Said* - a more grounded, realistic version of the genre, with Julia Louis-Dreyfus and James Gandolfini as divorced single parents taking a chance on each other. Terrific movie.

Woody Allen’s *Annie Hall* - Romcom for nerds who quote philosophers. Keep an eye out for Jeff Goldblum in an early, one-line bit part (it’s a great line).

Speaking of… *The Tall Guy*, an insanely underknown movie with Goldblum and Emma Thompson and Rowan Atkinson. Funniest sex scene ever.

I’m of a mind that classical Hollywood did sophisticated fluff for grown ups much better than current Hollywood. Especially if Cary Grant stars: *The Awful Truth, My Favorite Wife, Holiday, His Girl Friday, Bringing Up Baby, Charade.* That last one is blended with suspense-thriller, but it’s definitely got the vibe you’re looking for. (Audrey Hepburn: “You know what’s wrong with you?” Cary: “What?” Audrey: *”Nothing.*)

Other greats from that era:
Easy Living
The Lady Eve
No Man of Her Own
Mr. and Mrs. Smith (a rare non-thriller by Alfred Hitchcock - he directed at the request of star Carole Lombard, a personal friend of Hitch and his wife)

If you’re game for musicals, most from that era were essentially romcoms with some singing and dancing. Particular recommendations:

Singin’ in the Rain
Meet Me in St. Louis
Swing Time
The Pirate
An American in Paris
9. As good as it gets (1997)
10. The Cutting Edge
Mr. Right
Just Like Heaven
Muriel’s Wedding
Strictly Ballroom

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Tim Curry turns 80 today. Happy birthday to the legend!

**Score:** 2665

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|mlGCsBLl4luyQaOl8w)
  - His finest role. I choose to believe his continued excellence is simply an insatiable, but impossible, desire to top this performance.
2. ![gif](giphy|AvRDaSedmbW0M)

I absolutely adore this man.
3. ![gif](giphy|3o84U0gIUZ8WMZHzmE)


4. I'm the butler. I butle. 
5. Such a legend!

"Hiya Georgie, want a balloon?"
6. ![gif](giphy|BomJoHOcRgCti)


7. ![gif](giphy|Fgl7OyFKx0I7e)
8. ![gif](giphy|RQQkGMUaekt6o)

say it!!

Happiest of birthdays to an absolute legend (pun intended) 🎉🎉🎉
9. Ha, I just watched the episode of Tales From the Crypt where he play the mother, father, and daughter(that's the fourth pic in drag) i was just watching old episodes and noticed his name 3 times in the intro credits

## 2. Charlize Theron Says ‘In 10 Years, AI Is Going to Be Able to Do’ Timothée Chalamet’s Job, but it ‘Will Not Be Able to Replace’ Live Performance Like Ballet

**Score:** 376

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Actually this sparked an interesting idea. what if Ai means that plays come back? Like, instead of one theater in your town that actually does live performances, now there are several and because of AI backlash, there are less cinemas. And as a result, stages become these big, insanely complicated productions. Maybe not replacing cinemas but, similar to vinyl and film, making a comeback because of the qualities it has that it's replacement doesn't. 
  - Undoubtedly it will. 

The rise of music piracy and streaming resulted in artists focusing more on income from live music and merch, as well as the resurgence of vinyl. 

Any change in technology or distribution changes consumption and tastes. 
  - Could work, would be like musicians touring countries but obviously with actors.

Well, until some company realises they’re making money and decides they want in and release synthetic androids to do it and ruin that too.
    - You say that like actors don't already "tour", playing the same play in multiple cities...

 They do in my country at least.
2. The point of chalamet’s clearly true statement was that no one watches ballet. You could have a billion robots doing a billion ballet performances but who would watch?

It’s endlessly hilarious that society said “uh actually ballet is way bigger than movies and not dead at all. Here look at all these famous movie stars who say ballet is relevant. Oh yeah and here’s a bunch of anonymous struggling ballet professionals on their little tiktok accounts getting less than a million views. But did you hear what this actress said?”

Boston symphony orchestra is having its final season and I haven’t heard or seen a single thing about it outside of the niche local music community. People care more about an actor’s offhand comment on ballet than they do about opera, ballet, classical music, etc all combined. And it’s not even close.
  - Millions of people seem to gone to the mattresses for Ballet of all things.  Chalamet wasn’t wrong, especially because he was talking about financial stability.
  - I dont know how this blew up like it did lol.  Alot of people pretending they care about ballet.
3. more repost bots
4. Im sure Boston Dynamics has a robot that can already do Ballet
5. Thing is - robots can absolutely do ballet. It will only be a decision of the consumer what will be successful

## 3. Can this guy be in the next John Wick movie? Please.

**Score:** 342
**Body:** Manito "El Mariachi" from Desperado (1996)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robert Rodriguez must have the weirdest career I've ever seen. Dude has Alita, Spy Kids, Sin City, Star Wars and the Mexico trilogy on his resume.
  - He refuses to be pigeon holed!
2. These movies where john wick before there was a john wick
  - Juan Wick 
3. This was John wick before John wick
4. LOVE this film, and Antonio Banderas as El Mariachi!!!  And Salma was fantastic and so gorgeous!
5. ![gif](giphy|fNm0g7dy2cciQ)
6. "Can't you people see that we are fucking CLOSED?"
  - 'WHAT IS THE NUMBER!? TO THE PHONE IN MY CAAAaar!
7. I have a soft spot for the glorious absurdity of the Guitar that is also a shotgun AND machine gun, with enough shells and bullets to take out an entire bar full of guys.

## 4. Whats the most rewatchable movie of all time and why is it Clue (1985)

**Score:** 291
**Body:** Happy 80th birthday to the absolute legend himself Tim Curry ❤️

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I "bought" this movie in 2015 on Amazon- watched it a ton and then went to watch it one day, during covid, and was prompted to re purchase the movie.. thats when I learned you are renting movies for an extended period of time, not buying the digital copy, for life...

  
still can't believe its legal in the US to label it as purchasing to own. 
  - Guess that’s why we have vSeeBox now
2. I didnt like the ending but i liked the ending but i hated the ending but i loved the ending but the ending was meh but the ending was great
  - And every ending works perfectly if you pay attention while watching 
3. https://i.redd.it/med2kvv186wg1.gif
4. For me it’s Office Space, after working an office job for the past decade it’s just perfect. 
5. Communism was a red herring
6. Not the cognac, just in case…
  - Well...I HAD to stop her screaming. 
7. The Leslie Nelson Suite and Eurotrip are also on my most rewatchable list too

## 5. Happy birthdays to Hayden Christensen and Simu Liu!! Hayden is now 45 years old and Simu is now 37 years old. What are your favorite characters they played?

**Score:** 206
**Body:** For Hayden, Anakin Skywalker in the Star Wars prequel trilogy series and for Simu, it’s Shang Chi. Can’t wait to see Simu again in Avengers: Doomsday 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Simu got fucked over so badly by Marvel. He hasn’t shown up since Shang Chi and I have a bad feeling they are going to kill him off in Doomsday. 
  
But my fave performance from him is from Kim’s Convenience. 
  
And I think Hayden was great in RotS.
  - I couldn't take my eyes off Simu when watching the Barbie movie. He seemed so genuine and into the role, and he was electric during the dance scene. It seemed like he enjoyed being there
2. https://preview.redd.it/89axnocu65wg1.jpeg?width=736&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=d205a86cab123dc9f2d79e1f496c97f04f2c7191

Sam Monroe
  - https://i.redd.it/4hr74btj58wg1.gif
3. Stephen Glass
4. Happy Birthday Lord Vader 
5. Jumper
6. Is HC turning in to John Schneider? lol 
7. Hayden as Anakin is one of those roles that gets better the more years pass.
8. They need to put Simu in a Star Wars show. I’d nominate him as a member of Rogue Squadron.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Directors directly influenced by Tarkovsky

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Hey everyone, I’m trying to write a paper on Tarkovsky and I can’t find many examples of filmmakers directly influenced by him, yet i do see a lot of people mentioning his very important and relevant legacy, having inspired a lot of directors that came later. 

I’ve only seen Lars Von Trier mentioned, having dedicated Antichrist to Tarkovsky.

If anyone knows any or can help clarify, I’d really appreciate the help. Many thanks.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bi Gan has cited Tarkovsky as one of his biggest influences, with *Stalker* specifically being the film that inspired him to pursue filmmaking.

If Resurrection (2025) is showing near you I'd highly recommend it.

I haven't been able to see either of Bi Gan's other films but I know they've both been compared to Tarkovsky's work, so I think he's a director worth looking into.
  - In Long Day's Journey Into Night, there's clear influence from both Solaris and Stalker, if not other Tarkovsky works. It's the only film of Bi Gan's I've seen, very much want to see Resurrection and Kaili Blues.
2. Alejandro Gonzalez Iñárritu has stated that the first time he saw an Andrei Tarkovsky movie, he was completely shocked and amazed by it. He often cites Andrei Rublev as his favorite movie, and it alongside his entire filmography were direct influences on The Revenant. It's also possible that a movie like Mirror influenced Iñárritu's own Bardo, as both are non-traditional semi-autobiographical stories focused on time and memory.

[https://goldenglobes.com/articles/tarkovsky-and-revenant-homage-and-beyond/](https://goldenglobes.com/articles/tarkovsky-and-revenant-homage-and-beyond/)

Honestly, you can just go to Tarkovsky's Wikipedia page, scroll down to "reception and legacy", and find countless directors who cite Tarkovsky as an inspiration. Check their references for good measure.
  - Here's the classic [Revenant by Tarkovsky](https://vimeo.com/153979733) video, for those who haven't seen it.
3. In addition to Lars Bon Trier: Ingmar Bergman, Akira Kurosawa, Nuri Bilge Ceylon, Krzysztof Kieślowski, Terrence Malick, Béla Tarr, Alejandro González Iñárritu, Denis Villeneuve, Christopher Nolan. 

Some of these you can find quotes from about their direct influence from the director or specific films. 
  - It's interesting that a lot of the influence with these directors goes both ways.  The reason I say that is because I think I heard somewhere that Tarkovsky liked Ingmar Bergman's work, and Bergman said the same about Tarkovsky.  I'm pretty new to the deeper dives in film so I can't really be more specific.  I recently got the Ingmar Bergman box set from Criterion and started watching some of the films last week.
4. >Hey everyone, I’m trying to write a paper on Tarkovsky and I can’t find many examples of filmmakers directly influenced by him, yet i do see a lot of people mentioning his very important and relevant legacy, having inspired a lot of directors that came later.

maybe Nikita Mikhalkov and his brother Andrei Konchalovsky
5. >Hey everyone, I’m trying to write a paper on Tarkovsky and I can’t find many examples of filmmakers directly influenced by him, yet i do see a lot of people mentioning his very important and relevant legacy, having inspired a lot of directors that came later. 

 You can check out Nuri Bilge Ceylan for a clear cut example. You also have Kieślowski and Iñárritu, as well as Zvyagintsev.

Also, you can (and tbf also 'should') simply search in JSTOR or z-lib, and such for specific books and articles, and gather specific information if you're to write a paper.
6. Mamoru Oshii, best known for directing Ghost in the Shell (1995), has cited Tarkovsky as an inspiration. This can also be seen in angels egg and Patlabor 2, both have a cerebral philosophical tone, a heavy dosage of atmosphere, which is reflected in the sound design and in the music, as well as as stunning cinematography. One of the long takes in angels egg could be compared to the long take from nostalghia.
7. Alexander Sokurov is a very direct example. It's patently clear from the films, not in any slaveish way but the transcendental style, painterly approach, deep seriousness, pace and many themes. Beyond that, Tarkovsky acted as a mentor to him in various ways and called Sokurov a genius.

## 2. My Neighbor Totoro Review

**Score:** 12
**Body:** My Neighbor Totoro, as a kid, I feel like I would have enjoyed this a lot, but I don't know why I enjoyed it so much even after growing up. The way it portrays a child's perspective of the world is something I've hardly ever seen done so well in any other movie. Even though the stakes were low and the story was simple, it still felt magical. Seeing Totoro and all the other monsters was a lot of fun, and it also had a happy ending, overall, the movie was very optimistic.

​I also really liked the way the village was depicted in this film. The portrayal of Mei and Satsuki's father reminded me of my own parents, specifically how my dad used to take care of us whenever my mom fell ill. Mei's frustration when Totoro wouldn't appear, followed by him finally showing up, was all very well done.

​It also leaves you with a lingering doubt, why don't these magical beings appear in front of adults? Are they even real, or just a part of the children's imagination? Overall, it’s a very good movie that you can watch over and over again.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Honestly, children under three could just watch this movie repeatedly as their only film and be fine. As a parent who was exposed to this film hundreds of times, I never grow tired of it.
  - Funnily enough, there’s an anecdote where one friend of Hayao Miyazaki reached out to congratulate him for the film and told him they put the movie for their son multiple times a week and Miyazaki was apparently horrified. He hated the idea of children watching his movies lots of times instead of getting inspired to go out to explore nature.
    - Sadly, he is no one to take parenting advice from.
  - My kids watched it in repeat and I still love it it's our everybody be chill movie now
2. >I don't know why I enjoyed it so much even after growing up

Well, because it's a great movie, I'd imagine. Ghibli movies are made for adults just as much as they are for kids (if not even more so, in some cases).
3. It's a magical film, one of the all time greats of children's entertainment. There's not really an overarching plot; there are some things that happen, but they're only loosely connected; in another film, that would drive me nuts, but in Totoro it's perfect. It's just a *nice* film, and one that's full to the brim of delights for children to discover. 


There's some levity from the mom being sick in the hospital, but it's never played for tears or stress. They never say what she's ill with, but Miyazaki's mother was in the hospital for tuberculosis when he was young, and that's often what people assume the mom has, a personal touch from the director.


I didn't grow up with this film, I didn't see it til well until adulthood, but I made sure to show it to my daughter. We held a movie night for her and her friends, and one of them doesn't really watch movies because she's easily upset by conflict, and I immediately thought of Totoro and put that on for them. She still wasn't sure about coming, and we assured her she could just eat and then leave anytime she wanted if she wasn't enjoying the movie, but instead she absolutely loved it and stayed the whole time. I hope that we've helped ease her into film as a medium, and that it will be a source of enjoyment for her in the future in a way that it hadn't been before. 
  - >There's some levity from the mom being sick in the hospital, but it's never played for tears or stress. 

One of the things I noticed last time I watched Totoro is how a lot of the story revolves around the childhood experience of people not being where they should be.

It starts with moving to a different place. The girls' mother being in hospital is not really a *worry* for them, it's more that she's not where she should be. Mei disappears into the undergrowth. Mei comes to school rather than staying at home. Their dad isn't there to pick them up when he should be. Mei goes missing (looking for their mum).
4. Interesting factoid about this movie. 

It was originally released as the back half of a double feature with Grave of the Fireflies. 

I guess they needed one of the all-time great feel good movies as a palate cleanser for one of the saddest movies of all-time. 
  - Intereating fact about the word factoid is that it means something falsely believed by people

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (April 19, 2026)

**Score:** 8
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Devil is Busy** (2024) \*\* Oscar-nominated short about a day in the life of the director of a small abortion clinic in Atlanta, where a few fanatical Christian protesters spend their days outside with a bullhorn trying to persuade people from entering. There's nothing revelatory or remarkable about it, and aside from getting the nomination, there's not much reason to see this.

**The Boy and the Heron** (2023) \*\*\* All the weird and surreal Miyazaki stuff is there, but there's no entryway to bring you into the story. It's basically about a teenage boy trying to find his lost stepmother, but the motivation is as minimal as can be, and thanks to the expressionless animation, the boy has almost no reaction to all the fantastic things going on around him, so you don't know what he thinks about any of it or if it's weird or do you care that much (contrast this with any of the "ugly" or non-human characters, who are full of reactions and emotions). So yes, the animation is lovely, but I was completely unengaged by the story, which seemed to be full of rules I wasn't privy to. 

**About Time** (2013) \*\*\* Seems like as a storyteller you'd want to use time travel to create problems, not fix them. Every time something bad happens in this film, he just time travels and fixes it, and there's no stakes, no complications, and no fun. It also completely ignores the ethical dilemmas of how he manipulates those around him, or how he himself might have been manipulated by those around him, or how he doesn't actually deal with his problem - sort of the opposite of Groundhog's Day. Instead, it leans into the idea of living with your mistakes and valuing the time you have, which is a good way to go and apparently this resonates deeply with fans of the film, but I don't think the first half sets any of that up well and everything is muddled because his perfect, wonderful life only happens because he fixes all his mistakes and doesn't make him a better person. I was also frustrated that after his first big blunder (losing her phone number) he didn't do the one very obvious thing to fix it, which is go back to when she gave him the number. That said, it's a lot more tolerable than other Richard Curtis movies despite the schmaltz, a filmmaker I futilely keep holding out hope for just because he worked on *Black Adder* and *Mr. Bean*.

**Donnie Darko** (2001) \*\*\*\* Is *Donnie Darko* the last of the 90s cynical Gen X disaffected with suburbia and the American dream movies before 9/11 changed our perspective on world affairs? I saw this when it came out and I wasn't as impressed as a lot of people. This time I was hoping to see more of a connection with *Harvey*, but it's probably closer to *Equus*. I like that it's weird and how the convoluted story turns in on itself, but there are also too many characters with weak relationships and story threads to follow. I especially wanted him to talk to the old woman, but that seems to go nowhere. It doesn't feel complete, so maybe I should check out the director's cut, even though fans say it removes all the mystery.

**Thief of Bagdad** (1940) \*\*\* The film is notable for being the first color feature to use blue screen, but boy is the effect terrible to modern eyes, and they use it a lot. It's a lavish and colorful spectacle, but it's also very noisy with music constantly playing over the dialogue, which made it monotonous to watch. Sabu is a charming street urchin, but his companion and main character John Justin is wooden and charmless. This would be such a better movie with a different lead (like the athletic Fairbanks in the 1924 version). Consequently, Conrad Veidt steals the movie as Jaffar. The story is rough as well. The Djinn in the bottle just happens out of the blue as if it's only there to move the plot along, but mostly what hurts the film is that the effects are pretty terrible despite being inventive. Perhaps it's good enough to entertain kids, but I had to remind myself that this was a year after Wizard of Oz, and now I'm more impressed at how fully realized and convincing Oz's fantasy world truly was. 
2. **Tampopo (1985, Juzo Itami)**

This was a fun watch, loved all of the characters and vignettes woven throughout the film. Tampopo was the star, with her ramen shop glow up. I had a bowl of tonkotsu ramen before the showing and it made for the perfect dinner and a movie! **6/10**

**The Drama (2026, Kristoffer Borgia)**

Glad to have avoided any spoilers for this one. Was planning on watching once it hit streaming but curiosity got the best of me, plus I really like both Zendaya and Robert Pattinson. I liked this film a lot overall, they had great chemistry and there was lots of dark humor throughout. **7/10**

**History of the World: Part I (1981, Mel Brooks)**

Mel Brooks as writer, director, and actor! I think this is the first of his that I’ve seen and it’s probably because I wasn’t in the mood for this type of film, but I really didn’t enjoy it. Just not my cup of tea. **4/10**

**The Game (1997, David Fincher)**

Michael Douglas was great as the leading role, but this film fell flat for me. It’s possible that I went in with high expectations based on glowing reviews but the twists weren’t that suspenseful and it didn’t hit that sweet spot of a thriller for me. **6/10**
3. (I went a bit crazy again this week.) [**WEEK # 276:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

**8 MORE PALESTINIAN MOVIES:**

🍿 *“Daddy, what is Deir Yassin?…”*

**ALL THAT'S LEFT OF YOU** (2025), my second by Cherien Dabis (After 'Make a wish'), and the **saddest movie I've seen all month.**

An epic family saga, tracing three tragic Palestinian generations from 1948 to 2022, reminded me of Bertolucci's 'Novecento'. How ordinary people become refugees, and the sufferings they continue to endure for 70 years. The misery, injustice, and brutalities was nearly impossible to bear. But it was told with humanity and warmth, without politicizing, and was extremely well-made. [The trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=obY98vk76Hs). **9/10.**

One of these film experiences that is very unpleasant to see, but if I won't watch them, who will?

💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **THE TIME THAT REMAINS** (2009) by Elia Suleiman is similar to 'All that's left of you', in that he too tells the history of one family from 1948 until the present time. But the family is "Israeli-Arab", and not dispossessed refugees, so their lot as second-class citizens is humiliating but not lethal (for the most parts). It's told in a symbolic, theatrical exposition style,  which reminded me a bit of Roy Andersson absurdist tableaux (just like his 'It must be heaven'). It uses lots of local tropes, the olive groves, the dusty, terraced hills, the street cafés... 

[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rp0MuEsUptM) 4/10. 

🍿 *"There is escape. And there is the struggle. And she is stuck between the two..."*

**FERTILE MEMORY** (1980), my 2nd by Michel Khleifi (after the fantastic 'Tale of the Three Jewels'.) This was his first documentary, and the first ever feature to be shot inside the occupied territories. A poignant story of two different Palestinian women: One was an old widow in Israel itself, whose family's land was stolen and who's left with nothing, and the other, a divorced novelist from Nablus in the West bank must fend for herself and forge her own female identity. What does it mean to be an ordinary Palestinian, and live under brutal occupation for three generations? It's tough when the land was everything and now it is gone. And still you must persist. Pain and beauty. 7/10.

🍿 **A BOY, A WALL, AND A DONKEY** (2008), my first short by (twice) Oscar-nominated director Hany Abu-Assad. 3 young Arab village kids want to shoot a gangster film, but they have no camera. **8/10.** Next I'm going to watch his 'Paradise now' and 'Omar'!

*(These 3 directors above were all born in the little town of Nazareth between 1950 and 1961! And I was born in 1953, 30 kilometers away).*

🍿 **PALESTINIAN WOMEN** (1974) is a short documentary by Lebanese Jocelyne Saab that gave voice to the movement of revolutionary, militant, Fedayeen women of that time. It was commissioned for French television, but was never shown in public. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **SOUND OF THE STREET** (2006), an early, impressionistic short by Annemarie Jacir (After 'Palestine 36'). Ants are running around on a marble slate, while pedestrians are discussing the news of the day. Meh. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **YOUR FATHER WAS BORN 100 YEARS OLD AND SO WAS THE NAKBA** (2018) is another short visual poem about the soul of the refugee wandering the streets of old Haifa, her birth place, via Google Street View. Even though my own father lived in the same streets as a child in the 1930's, it's not evocative enough. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **NIGHTMARE OF GAZA** (2018), my second by Farah Nabulsi (after 'The Present'). An experimental poem of a woman whose world is destroyed. But it's in English, and not very good. *[Female Director.]*

*General observations:*

* Many of these movies featured the Palestinian star Saleh Bakri ('The Blue Kaftan', 'Costa Brava, Lebanon').

* It looks like there's a high number of female directors among the 620 movies which were made in Palestine through the years. 

* Of all the Palestinian movies that I've seen, here are my Top Four: 'All that's left of you' (above) and 'Make a wish' by Cherien Dabis, Farah Nabulsi's 'The Present', and 'Salt'. 

* The inhuman occupation and brutal genocide by the apartheid state of Israel must come to an end. 🇵🇸 **Free Palestine.**

I have another batch of Palestinian movies that I want to see, but I’ll wait to see them next week, inshallah.

🍿 

**2 FROM LEBANON:**

🍿 *“I want to sue my parents. Because I was born.”*

Nadine Labaki's award winning **CAPERNAUM** (2018) holds the top spot on Letterboxd out of all 1,044 films which were produced in Lebanon. It's a depressing, devastating tale of neglect and abuse in the poorest slums of Beirut, played so realistically that I regret watching it. Suffering and misery, so much misery, anchored around one resilient street boy who must survive the cruel streets of hopelessness. The parents who sold their 11 yo daughter for two chickens as soon as she got her first period, the emigrant mother who was arrested and had to abandon her baby, the street boy who had to care for a baby not his own... Everything about it was difficult to watch - except for the film-craft. The 12-yo Syrian refugee who played the lead so authentically, was himself discovered living in the streets. 

[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ULUo0048xZE) **It's a must watch masterpiece - but it comes with a warning.** *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **SUBMARINE** (2016), my second by Mounia Akl, a dystopian end-of-the-world allegory. It seems like a prelude to her terrific 2021 'Costa Brava, Lebanon', which likewise depicted a grim, near-future Beirut overrun by tons of garbage, toxic rain, social collapse and desperation. Everybody tries to escape, except for one defiant young woman. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

**STATUS AND TERRAIN** (2019) is an unusual German documentary about the early, small concentration camps that the Nazi regime started building right after its 1933 takeover. 

It contains only two elements: For 2 hours, the camera records ordinary architecture, buildings, houses, streets and parks, in dull, provincial little towns in Saxony and Thuringia. And a dry, monotone female voice reads excerpts from memos, letters and reports written by low-level officials describing the bureaucratic process of setting up these early facilities. The effects of these two are hypnotic. There is no music, overarching ideologies, explanations or points made, just a list: Somebody orders 3,000 low-quality blankets to be delivered at a certain date. Somebody makes a written note that the screams of tortured communists at the newly-established prison on the outskirts of town are too loud and disturb the neighbors. Things like that. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZvV8bzM5pqg)

Not as compelling as Claude Lanzmann's 'Shoah', the endless list of mundane acknowledgements, directives, requests for inspections, office correctives, and other tools of oppression is chilling. I also didn't realize that the Nazis built over 1,000 concentration camps during these 12 years, most of which inconspicuously blend in the neighborhoods, (and have since then reverted back to act as community centers, city halls, markets and schools.) 

To realize how similar it is to what trump's Department of Homeland Security is building right now across America is even more disturbing. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

**THE SNOWS OF KILIMANJARO** (2011), my first inspirational film by class-conscious French director Robert Guédiguian, and another random pick with 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. It tells of an ordinary, decent working class couple from Marseilles, a middle-age trade union rep at a shipyard and his wife, who are robbed and brutally beaten one night. The simple story is told without flair, just like the couple, but as the story unfolds, it turns into a humane Mike Leigh social realism gem. Apparently, it was inspired by a Victor Hugo poem 'How good are the poor'. [The trailer.](https://www.imdb.com/video/vi3100683545/?playlistId=tt1852006&ref_=tt_ov_ov_vi)

[This song](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DWxgpmxOBpI) plays a significant role in the plot. Recommended. 

*(Continued below)*
  - *(Part 2)*

My 8th by Yasujirō Ozu, **THE ONLY SON** (1936), his first sound film feature. A poor widow who works at a silk production factory, sacrifices everything just so her son can get a high school education, and he promises her that he will become 'a great man'. Thirteen years later she travels to Tokyo to visit him, only to realize that his life did not turn out the way they both had hoped for. It's simple and bitter-sweet at the same time. 

🍿 

Bo Widerberg's **ELVIRA MADIGAN** was a huge hit when it premiered in 1967, and [Mozart's andante from his Piano Concerto](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVKpvD3X6EM) became a plague and an ear-worm everywhere. It's an illicit, doomed romance of a beautiful, young couple, he a married deserter officer from the Swedish army and she a circus slack-dancer, and they elope into the gorgeous Danish countryside in the 1890's. They make love in the shade of trees, chase butterflies, and have picnics in the meadows with cheese and vine - that is until their meager savings run out. Soft-core romanticism. 

🍿 

**GABRIEL OVER THE WHITE HOUSE** was a curious pre-Code political message film that was produced with the help of William Randolph Hearst just before FDR inauguration. President Walter Huston nearly dies in a car accident, and is visited by an angel ("Gabriel"), who causes him to care about ordinary people, employment, social justice and world peace. But in order to pass muster in 1933, this nearly-socialist vision has to portray POTUS's behavior as a wannabe "Dictator". So politically it's murky. Franchot Tone gets second billing as his secretary. The gangster "Nick Diamond" looks exactly like real-life Roy Cohn. 

🍿 

**FREQUENT WATCHES X 3 (+1):**

🍿 *"...OK.... Sell it all..."*

**THE BIG SHORT** (2015) is my favorite by Adam McKay (with the exception of [THE LANDLORD](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SIdxVR_7ikg), the only one where Will Ferrell performance was tolerable). Because I devoured Michael Lewis non-fiction books then, and because it reminds me of my own professional life during the go-go 2000's.

Standout smaller performances by John Magaro, Timothy Spall's son (Rafe), Jeremy ("Kendall Roy") Strong, Melissa Leo, and naked Margot Robbie in a bubble bath, explaining mortgage-backed securities. I'm surprised that my friend disproved how closely this story resembled 'The wolf of Wall Street', one of his favorite movies. ♻️

🍿 *“For relaxing time, make it Suntory Time.”*

"Sleepless in Shinjuku", marketed under the commercial name of **LOST IN TRANSLATION** (2003), a perfect 10/10 mood piece about loneliness and connection in a globalized world. From its opening shot of 17-yo Scarlett Johansson's literal ass emerging from her dream, to the final scene when Bill Murray's Bob Harris whispers parting words in her ear to which we are not privy, it's a sublime exploration of contrasts, between cultures and personalities. Mysteries of two lost people stuck in a foreign space, alienated and displaced. A flawless romance that can never be. 

Definitely, Bill Murray's best role, and husband Giovanni Ribisi's as the most ass-holey. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 **CALL ME BY YOUR NAME** (2017), a same-sex coming of age romance, a sun-drenched mood of the Italian countryside of our most seductive fantasies, and Timothée Chalamet break-though role as a 17 yo intellectual twink. It was actually Chalamet's "Elio" who instigated the summer affair. The second half dragged a bit, and Michael Stuhlbarg's open-heart father-son talk at the end was a bit much, but all in all, it's still a beautiful, simple love story. ♻️

🍿 A lovely bonus from Luca Guadagnino, **THE LOVE FACTORY** (2002). It's an intimate, luxurious 35-min conversation with one of his favorite muse and co-worker, Tilda Swinton. He asks her generic, open-ended questions about risk-taking, performances, loneliness, and love, and she's comfortable enough to "go in 15 indifferent directions", philosophizing and digging deep into herself for answers. She's a delight, when then she sings Cat Stevens in Gaelic. She should play Bowie in a film. 

🍿 

*"Love isn't enough..."*

Another wistful gay love story, **PAIN AND GLORY** (2019). It's my 4th colorful drama by Almodóvar, an aesthete Spanish auteur I could never love 100% (maybe because I'm not refined enough). Antonio Banderas is a famed old film director, struggling with various debilitating ailments, and looking back at his life: His beautiful mother Penélope Cruz, his lovers, addictions and his fame, as well as his mortality. It seems like another indulgent personal journey, but it has quite a few moments of beauty and transcendence. 

🍿 

**SPIKE JONZE + LANCE BANGS X 3:**

🍿  *"How do you explain that you love somebody or something?"*

**TELL THEM ANYTHING YOU WANT: A PORTRAIT OF MAURICE SENDAK** (2009) is a wonderful, wonderful conversation with the then-80 yo author-illustrator. He talks a lot about his love for his brother and sister, how much he was affected at age 2 by the Lindbergh kidnapping, his lifelong morbid attachment to the idea of death, Etc. I was not aware that he composed over 100 children's books, and how gorgeous his illustrations were. Now I want to read all of them!

**Highly, highly recommended!** (And too bad that Jonze's adaptation of 'Where the wild things are' didn't capture the magic of the book). 

🍿  **HER: THE UNTITLED RICK HOWARD PROJECT** (2014) is how you do a 'Movie in the making'. If you loved the melancholic 'Her' the first couple of times, this will make you want to re-watch it again. A perfect companion piece.

🍿 **TO DIE BY YOUR SIDE** (2011) is an animated short using embroidered characters from classic novels. When the 'Shakespeare & Company' bookstore closes at night, the book-covers come to life. Including a stop motion sex scene.

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 **PETTICOAT CAMP** (1912. 15 min.) was an unusual feminist story about gender roles. A group of friends go camping, and the women soon realize that the men are having fun fishing and hunting, while they expect the women to do all the cooking and cleaning. Well, the women rebel and take off, and the men end up surrounding and agreeing to compromise. [Girl Power!](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Petticoat_Camp_%281912%29.webm) My first with major silent star Florence La Badie. 

🍿 "Steven Soderbergh’s **UNCHARTED SYMPHONY No. 1** (2014. 8 min.) offers us a conundrum: how do we discuss something that does not exist and--according to its author--will never exist? Is it appropriate to speculate about the author’s intention in announcing he would not be starting a symphony, when we have no result to react to? Can we address the former while acknowledging there is no latter? Should we?"

Abstract visuals. **9/10.**

🍿 **IMAGES OF OSTEND** (1929. 11 min.), my first poetic documentary by pioneer Belgian author Henry Storck. It's a wordless portrait of the famous local beach, one in a series. Storck was an actor in Jean Vigo's 'Zéro de conduite' (1933) in the role of the priest, as well as Chantal Akerman's 'Jeanne Dielman, 23 Quay Commercial, 1080 Brussels' (1975) in the role of a customer of the prostitute... Bonus surprise shot of a black cat in the dunes. O-key...

🍿 **EUPHORIA** (No, not that one! 1974, 3 min) by Vince Collins. If Peter Max and MC Escher "had sex on LSD and had a baby" while Santana was watching them, that underground baby would be this hallucinogenic Yellow Submarine hoot. 

🍿 **THE TREE AND THE CAT** (1983. 9 min.) is a lovely Ukrainian animation, in the style of the old Soviet kids fare. An abandoned cat learns how to be independent from a tree that doesn't need anybody. 

🍿 **A HEATHEN BENEFIT** (1918. 5 Min), my first of the Katzenjammer Kids cartoons, and **KRAZY KAT AND GNATZ MOUSE AT THE CIRCUS** (1916. 3 min), early, primitive animated cartoons.

Many of the films you watched deal with people living through the conflict in the Middle East and made me think of a film I saw long ago:  **The Band's Visit** (2007).  Have you seen it?

4. **I Know Where I’m Going** directed by Powell & Pressburger (UK/1945) - As World War II draws to a close, a polished and strong-willed young woman travels from Manchester, England to the wilds of Western Scotland to be married to an older, wealthy gentleman who lives on the Isle of Kilkoran.  Bad weather strands her on the nearby, rather rustic Isle of Mull (also trying to get to Kilkoran is a Navy officer on leave and three professional bagpipers who were hired to perform at the wedding ceremony).  Her several-day-stay among the many isolated, but sensible and decent, locals she meets makes her begin to question if she really wants to marry her somewhat pretentious fiancé (who is already on the island, bearing the storm delay with more patience than she is).  As her romantic feelings for the Navy officer grow, she decides to flee and recruits a local boy to risk ferrying her to her destination during the somewhat lessened, but still dangerous gale.

This is a great little film featuring some fantastic shots of the rough natural environment of the Hebrides and, like many motion pictures of that era, each supporting role is a character with a genuine personality (some a bit eccentric).  I didn’t think the earliest scenes (introducing our protagonist and her train trip from Manchester) were very well done, but once the film gets to Mull and everything slows down, the directors are in their element.  There’s pretty much everything a viewer could want here:  nice performances, a couple of castles, dogs, an ancient curse, bagpipe music, an eagle, some serious suspense, and lots and lots of water. 



 
  - That sounds lovely.

I'll watch it. Thank you.
5. **Less Than the Dust (1916, dir. John Emerson)**

Unsurprisingly, this movie about the white Mary Pickford playing an Indian is pretty racist and orientalist. India is painted as a supremely exotic locale full of poor ruffians and uncouth knaves who need to be kept in check by noble British soldiers. Some Indians stage a rebellion, though it's motivated primarily by superstitions over smallpox vaccines instead of something meatier like religious or political repression. Pickford's character ostensibly sides with a charismatic British officer in the midst of this, ideologically aligning the film with a racist status quo. It's not endearing, and it's not even very interesting. In ignoring the racism, the story of this movie is very plain. Pickford's motivations are inconsistent and often unclear, at least until the last third when the movie takes a sharp turn.

I've referred to Pickford's character as an Indian, but this is not really the case. While she's been raised from birth by an Indian blacksmith in an Indian city, her parents were both white Englishman, and she's been separated from her homeland all her life. This is given little setup, and doesn't recontextualize much of the preceding story. Although she's supposed to be white, I'm still pretty sure Mary Pickford was in brownface in this movie. Obviously you can't tell for sure because the movie is in black-and-white, but her skin definitely has an oily tinge that isn't common in Mary Pickford movies where she's playing a lily white American. As a result of all of this, I'm inclined to believe that this twist was a late innovation in the making of the film. Our characters are transported from India to an English country manor in the last thirty minutes, and all of a sudden we're in a convoluted melodrama about whether or not Mary Pickford is due to inherit the wealth of some old lord who happens to be related to Pickford's love interest (ew). Themes of colonial violence and culture go out the window as Less Than the Dust turns into a turgidly paced Pygmalion clone. The last scene of the movie shows a bunch of Englishmen dragging Pickford back into the house because English manors are too hot. It's a very bizarre creative choice. It's not as offensive as the first hour, but it doesn't redeem the movie. If anything, it makes Less Than the Dust a lot more disjointed.

Less Than the Dust's saving graces are few, but they are consistent and worth noting. While this is not a great look for Mary Pickford, she performs her role admirably and does not lack for charm. The recreated Indian city and temples are beautiful, and Emerson shoots them masterfully. With the right script, this could have been a really gripping epic, but as it is it's sloppy and leaves a bad taste in your mouth.

**Benedetta (2021, dir. Paul Verhoeven)**

As gripping and fun as Verhoeven's American sci-fi trilogy is, what makes those movies work are their cerebral stories and themes. All of that is present in spades in Benedetta, a movie that is decidedly not American sci-fi schlock. It looked at the intersection of faith and sexuality in a really interesting and nuanced way. Instead of Bible-thumpers screeching Leviticus at meek gay people, Benedetta's commentary comes from core Catholic ideas about physicality and supernatural experiences and the ways in which people are raised in dogmatic societies. When you're told to shun your body and to venerate people who had supernatural experiences, it's only natural that wires will get crossed when you do get supernatural visions that tell you to embrace the human body. I love Benedetta herself and how assertive and ambitious she is, and the more antagonistic characters who care more about the political ramifications of Benedetta's meteoric rise to near-sainthood than what she's doing with the cute novice. Homophobia doesn't even come up until the third act, and even then that's just another card in the villain's deck. Benedetta roots the Catholic Church's views on homosexuality in a very messy, complicated political context that still communicates strong messages about acceptance and identity. It's a very unique, compelling film.

**Little Mary Sunshine (1916, dir. Henry King)**

There are a few movies from the 1910s where someone adopts a child and they fix each other's personal problems (Rags, The Innocence of Ruth, Hoodoo Ann), and Little Mary Sunshine fits snugly into that niche. It doesn't do much more than the bare minimum storywise. Male father figure feels grounded by Mary's presence in his home, and he provides her with a stable home life. It's pretty unremarkable, and at a breezy 46 minutes it's hard to muster much one way or the other about it. Its only exceptional quality is the presence of Marie Osborne, the cute-as-a-button little girl who plays the titular Mary. She puts all of herself into every scene, whether she's getting ready for bed, playing with a bear cub, or watching her mother die. It's hard not to be endeared by her, and by proxy, to believe that the male father figure wouldn't love her even though he only knows her for a few days. Sometimes you got to put aside the heart-of-stone film snob and just enjoy seeing a little girl laugh at the world.

**Scent of a Woman (1992, dir. Martin Brest)**

I could not connect to this movie. I think a lot of it comes down to Al Pacino. He's the sole reason this film is remembered, so I was expecting a really gripping performance. It was not gripping. In the more downcast scenes, Pacino really shines, but for the most part he's just overdoing it. Sometimes he sounded like a drill sergeant in an SNL sketch, and other times he sounded like a white Morgan Freeman. I never bought him as a tortured, asocial vet, and I never liked him. I never even liked disliking him. He was just an asshole, but the movie and Chris O'Donnell had such an ineffable, profound connection to him that I had no choice but to watch him for 150 minutes. I like the premise of his arc, but I wanted the story and character to be totally different.

Outside of Frank Slade, I still didn't get much out of the movie. It revolves around an ethical debate O'Donnell is faced with: does he protect his asshole friends or tell the bad guy from the Seinfeld finale that they were responsible for a prank in exchange for a free Harvard admission? At no point did I ever think to consider the former option. The film fails to endear the viewer to O'Donnell's asshole friends, and even he knows that they're a bunch of no-good ingrates. Maybe if I'd watched this movie in high school I would have thought that his inner conflict was profound, but even at my still young age I know that a secure future is more important than high school friends. The film wants me to reconsider my position, though. Thematically, Scent of a Woman is deeply interested in an impulsive life, the kind of life where you squeeze more into eighty minutes than most people do in eighty years. It's a vague and flimsy message on its face, and the fact that it's being espoused by Pacino (even after O'Donnell helps him over his suicidal ideation hump) makes it seem all the more dubious. It's the kind of wishy-washy profundity that doesn't resonate if you bother to think about it for more than the time it takes to watch.

Overall, if you want a New England-set movie about the lives and ambitions of young men, stick with Dead Poets Society and Good Will Hunting.

**X2: X-Men United (2003, dir. Bryan Singer)**

Where X-Men was made in a time of relative political/economic stability in the US, X2 taps into the paranoia and prejudice spawned by 9/11 and the War on Terror. Its story has a lot more urgency and the subtext is a lot meatier. It's a darker film than X-Men without losing sight of its pulpy comic book roots, and elevates the scope and stakes without unraveling the story. The action is good, the effects are strong, and the writing is fun. It's a remarkably proficient superhero film in virtually every way.
  - **Resident Evil: Retribution (2012, dir. Paul WS Anderson)**

Paul WS Anderson reaches a new low in a film that desperately tries to pretend that it's tying everything together in a series made of some incomprehensible, rope-proof material. Resident Evil: Retribution continues the series tradition of discarding everything and making something new. Legacy villain Albert Wesker turns coat and now seeks to help Milla Jovovich fight Umbrella for reasons that are never made clear, employing the help of video game characters Ada Wong, Leon Kennedy, and Barry Burton, who battle armies of zombies in major cities. It's totally illogical and impossible to understand. Finding the right words to describe my feelings about it is a huge challenge. It's very entertaining, but at the same time it's too stupid to really think about or even register as a piece of cinema. It's awful, but at the same time, it's magnificent.

**The Texas Chain Saw Massacre (1974, dir. Tobe Hooper)**

The Texas Chain Saw Massacre lacks the technical artistry of other horror movies, but in this discrepancy it finds strength. The Texas Chain Saw Massacre is a very ugly film, but in a very candid way, like a documentary about an underserved part of the world you'd never heard about. The cars are cluttered, the news is grim, the fashion is gauche, exposed skin is slick with sweat, and the houses are in extreme states of disrepair, but make no mistake - they aren't abandoned. Through detailed and totally unappealing production design and filmmaking, Hooper succeeds masterfully at creating and maintaining a distinct atmosphere of claustrophobic, nauseating dread, and the scares deliver in spades. From the maniacal hitchhiker to his revenant of a grandfather, the villains of the film are terrifying and expertly performed. Their deeds are brutal, and are amplified by the disgusting, inescapable aesthetics. There's not even much gore, but Hooper so deeply immerses you in this world that that fact doesn't matter. This might as well be a Verhoeven movie at the end of the day. It's easy to see why so many filmmakers in the intervening decades have wanted to make their own slashers in the hopes of recreating Hooper's nightmarish masterpiece.

**Poor Little Peppina (1916, dir. Sidney Olcott)**

A cheap slurry of tropes from all over the 1910s cinema scene. Here and there are bits of Regeneration, The Italian, and even Less Than the Dust. The final result, burdened by a convoluted plot and frustratingly tepid direction, is an unremarkable film of no great merit. Steer clear of Poor Little Peppina. There are much better Pickford and Olcott films out there.

**A Simple Plan (1998, dir. Sam Raimi)**

A Simple Plan doesn't reach the dizzying heights of its sister film Fargo, but it's still a damn fine feature. It could have easily come off as a cheap studio cash-in on the Coens' film, but putting Raimi at the helm, a good friend of the Coens, was a very smart idea. A Simple Plan is a very character-driven film, looking less at the random cruelty of a chaotic universe and more at the way personalities clash when situations go south. Bill Paxton is excellent as a moral anchor trying and failing to keep himself and his friends in check as they navigate this very dangerous situation. Although he succumbs to the same evils that they do, you never lose sympathy for him. You want the same thing he does: for everyone to behave normally and keep cool heads. Billy Bob Thornton is a great foil for Paxton. He's caught up in a game of shifting alliances at first, but learns to take a stand by the end, which has tragic results. Although he's very unintelligent, he's not afraid to call out Paxton's bullshit and he's more observant than other characters will give him credit for, serving as a vessel for exposition that allows us to better understand Paxton. Brent Briscoe and Bridget Fonda are also excellent, and Raimi mostly restrains himself, directing in a naturalistic style that's more cinematic than the Coens' approach to Fargo. It's an undersung entry in his filmography, and I can't wait to watch it again one day.

## 4. Tourette’s, Possession, and Mary Shelley

**Score:** 1
**Body:** I keep seeing people say Ida in The Bride! “has Tourette’s,” and it’s wild to me because the film is extremely direct about what’s happening to her. In the opening, Mary Shelley literally say she’s looking for a “crack” she can slip into to tell this story, and lands on “possession.” Then we cut to Ida at the table drunk, people pleasing, being made to kiss another girl and swallow oysters, while Mary’s face presses against her mind and whispers “wake up, darling.”  From that point on, Ida’s repeating and stuttering read as a dead author forcing her way through a woman the world treats as disposable so she can finally give her Bride a voice, underlined by the black crystaloid fluid from the experiment which looks like “ink” staining her skin.  

At a certain point it doesn’t feel like genuine confusion; it feels like people would rather call it nonsense than sit with a very clear, very uncomfortable possession story the film is telling.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nobody thinks tourettes. that makes no sense. it’s clearly the spirit of Mary Shelley animating this woman for a short time to make outbursts.

i found it very offputting as a story device. the movie would have been much stronger without it. you could probably have left it on the cutting room floor and nobody would notice its absence.
  - I’ve just seen a lot of people toss that out as a lazy explanation instead of engaging with the very explicit possession setup. It feels like watching the movie with one eye open.  

For me, you can’t just cut the possession stuff without changing what the movie is about. Without Mary forcing her way in, Ida is just a sex worker who dies and comes back pretty much the same. Mary climbing into Ida is literally the reason we get a Bride at all: every time Mary takes over, the Bride doesn’t just sit there, she fights back, she kills the people trying to hurt her and Frank, she pushes against whatever role she’s being shoved into.
As the film goes on, we see less of Mary possessing her and more of Ida doing those same defensive moves and refusals on her own. Her identity as someone who prefers not to, who talks back and fights back, solidifies so much that by the end even Mary’s spirit can’t stay inside her. Ida is screaming for Mary and Mary doesn’t come; she makes that final choice alone for the first time.  To me that’s a complete trajectory
2. I didn't think what they were doing there was really successful to be honest and—while certainly a fun acting challenge for Buckley—really tedious to watch, especially without subtitles. 

I'm honestly much more interested in the universe travel/isekai sci-fi exploration of things they just casually drop in the dialogue like five minutes before the movie ends because that shit? Bonkers. Wish they'd actually gone somewhere with that.

Also, I totally get the folks calling it nonsense. There's so many choices and plot threads in this thing that just... dissolve into nothingness, wether due to studio interference or bad writing or whatever, that it's really difficult to even look at what was put out as a finished, well-thought out product. Naturally, that's gonna extend to the less than optimally handled plot device here.

## 5. I need some help for getting slow cinema.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I’m recently trying to get more into slow cinema out of curiosity, but everytime I sit down to watch one of these, I come out confused. 

I wasn’t necessarily bored, I understand slow cinema has more of an emphasis on emotions and contemplative thought, and avoids spelling its message out to its audience. But whenever I watch one of these, I simply begin to disassociate from the film out of frustration.

I often begin to question wether or not I actually liked the film, or if I am in denial just because other people like it. Sometimes, I just go to a YouTube video to help understand the film. Afterwards, I do see the point the film is going for, and can appreciate it, but a part of me also feels stupid whenever I do it. I look down on myself having to watch someone else’s analysis of a film in order to understand it rather than being able to analyze it on my own.

This particularly happened when watching “Funny Games” and “What Happened Was…”. They were good films but to be honest, I don’t really enjoy watching it. I only came to fully appreciate them a long while after watching it, and seeing someone else’s review of the film.

Then I tried watching Stalker and Long Day’s Journey Into Nights and… I straight just began to fall asleep midway through both films. I don’t want to say it was out of boredom, but at a certain point I just couldn’t concentrate on anything happening. Maybe it was because I watched then in the evening, but I literally don’t always have time to watch movies in the morning.

It’s not that I don’t get what it is going for, but I just sort of begin to just getting frustrated and confused, and no matter how much I try to keep myself from doing it, I end up falling into that same trap.

Got any tips to avoid this? Have you also felt this at some point? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The only real solution is to keep going. The more films you watch, the better you get at being able to analyze things on your own. 

That being said, you should never watch movies that genuinely don’t interest you. You might simply be realizing that slow cinema is not for you, and that’s perfectly fine. 
2. i wouldn’t rlly considered funny games slow cinema at all but, long days journey into night, stalker, and other “slow cinema” similiar to those, i think about kind of like paintings. paintings evoke emotion and tell a story not through plot but through imagery and subconscious meaning. will looking up the “meaning” of a painting help you understand it on a logical level? yeah sure. but i would argue this removes the joy and emotion you get when you really feel the meaning for yourself and connect with the art

One of my favorite filmmakers David Lynch has TONS of youtube theories dissecting his movies like they are a puzzle to solve, i mostly stay away from these as it ruins the magic for me and its not really what he was aiming to make (fun fact - he was originally a painter)


if you are getting bored and not getting anything out of certain types of films maybe they just aren’t for you at the end of the day. maybe you just don’t connect with it. 
3. You might have some burnout from using screens too much. Try reading a bit a day to improve your attention span. You could also try some foreign films that force you to pay attention because you have to read subtitles. 
  - I definitely have been trying to read more recently, so thanks for the encouragement.
4. for me the key was Twin Peaks. let David Lynch lull you into a false sense of security and then drop the atom bomb of FWWM on your head. Then The Return which leaves you baffled, angry even, but undeniably *curious*. Then you go back to Lynch's other stuff- Eraserhead, Mulholland Drive, and you start to develop an ear for this particular cinematic dialect, and the world of Twin Peaks opens up to you a second time in a whole new way. I don't think if I hadn't already been a Lynch fan, for example, that I would have been able to remotely tolerate Skinamarink or The Outwaters.
5. I’d suggest you watch whatever you think could be interesting.  Find a director you enjoy and go through their filmography to gain a wider perspective of their work. Chasing after a vibe or similar style of a film you enjoy is often a set up for disappointment. 

 I wasn’t aware of slow cinema as a genre, I just watch movies that interest me but it can be difficult scratching the itch sometimes.  Movies shouldn’t feel like eating your vegetables, so don’t sweat the tedious stuff.  Trash is equally important too. 

6. I’d suggest just watching more of it. Slow cinema is one of those things where, due to the nature of it, it’s more hit or miss than standard films. Either you really vibe with it or you don’t, even if you can appreciate what it’s going for. I personally found Stalker pretty boring, and liked Long Day’s Journey Into Night but also almost fell asleep several times - so it’s definitely not just you! You gotta dig until you find stuff that hits you right. Then once you’ve found one or two slow films that you enjoy, it can help you know what to look for or give you a deeper appreciation for other stuff. Here are some suggestions that may or may not help you find slow stuff that works for you/helps you appreciate other works in the style:

Inherent Vice - it’s like The Big Lebowski in slow motion. Very deliberately paced but very funny

Goodbye Dragon Inn - absolutely gorgeous, meditative, melancholy, but also with occasional flashes of humor. This movie is VERY slow, but also only about 90 minutes. 

Nicolas Winding Refn’s later films - I find these to be pretty slow but also accessible and with occasional bursts of very intense violence. If you like film noir you’d probably dig some of his work. 

Bela Tarr - if you watch some of his movies, it will permanently rewire your sense of what “slow” means. I think the slow pacing works both to convey tone and because it lets me think about what the meaning of what I’m seeing is. If his films were faster I wouldn’t really have time to untangle my ideas about meaning until later. Don’t start with Satantango obviously, I personally liked Werckmeister Harmonies the best and I think it’s a good intro to his style. 

Beau Travail - probably the most hypnotic film I’ve ever seen. It’s slow but it doesn’t really feel like it, just totally puts you in a trance.
7. I will say as someone who is internet poisoned and can’t sit still for a minute without looking at my phone. I love slow cinema, but I don’t go home and just pop in a Tsai Ming Liang and sit my ass down for two hours of Lee Kang sheng wondering around a city. I find watching it at a theatre is a lot more engaging than watching it at home. I suggest seeking out if your local art theatre has a showing for them
8. Bresson was the real slow cinema gateway drug for me. They are meditative but also really engaging, there's something magic in there that for me is a little closer to the surface than in some other slow cinema. I'd recommend Au Hazard Balthazar first, then maybe The Pickpocket, or A Man Escaped and then see if you want to keep going.

I'd also recommend if you're watching at home to try as much as you can to recreate a cinema experience and get rid of distractions. Of course if you can see any of these films in an actual movie theater it's going to be a totally different experience.

I was at a screening of a pretty solidly slow current film recently and the artist was there for a Q&A. He said that he didn't mind if people fell asleep during the movie, it's a reasonable reaction. So maybe just don't get frustrated with yourself and accept reactions.

If you want to get really into it, Paul Schrader's book Transcendental Style In Film gives a pretty solid analysis of how some of these films function.
9. “It’s not that I don’t get what it is going for, but I just sort of begin to just getting frustrated and confused, and no matter how much I try to keep myself from doing it, I end up falling into that same trap.

Got any tips to avoid this? Have you also felt this at some point?”

I think you jumped into the deep end too quickly! Definitely stretch yourself beyond your limits. Slow cinema is like a challenging novel. It’s going to test you a bit. But you don’t have to go all the way with Stalker right off the bat! Too much too early.
